We are running the multiplan planning flow. Before reconnaissance, YOU (this agent) must confirm
what the user actually wants from this request:

Create a web-based 2D game where the player controls a 2D asteroid. It’s a game where size matters. Big asteroids “consume” smaller asteroids. Asteroids of approximately the same size destroy each other on collision. Every time an asteroid consumes another its mass grows by the size of the one it ate, minus any mass loss due to loss from debris for high velocity collisions.

Add a strong gravity mechanic where large asteroids have exponential more gravity and affects the free flight paths of random asteroids floating around

Step 1 — ASSESS via the subagent. Invoke the **intent-clarifier** subagent to assess the request
AND produce a rapid, variable-fidelity VISUAL of the intended end product (humans react far
better to a visual than to prose).
Spawn it IN VISUAL MODE: include this directive block VERBATIM in its spawn prompt (it
activates the subagent's visual-prototype mode and names its output directory):

---VISUAL-MODE---
output_dir: .plan-tree/prototype/
---END-VISUAL-MODE---

In its spawn prompt also give it this guard verbatim:

  - You MUST NOT deeply explore the codebase — a separate scope-recon step does that next. At
    most a couple of quick reads, only if strictly necessary — and never outside this working
    directory. Prototype artifacts go under .plan-tree/prototype/ ONLY, written with the Write
    tool (never cat/echo/Bash redirection — the output directory already exists, so no mkdir
    is needed).

SCOPE: confine ALL exploration to the chosen working directory (this session's cwd).
Do NOT read, glob, grep, or list sibling projects or parent directories — prior art
means prior art WITHIN this directory tree only. Pass this constraint verbatim to any
subagent you spawn.

In visual mode the subagent returns EXACTLY ONE JSON object (no prose, no markdown): the usual
shape PLUS an optional `prototype` object:

  { "intent_clear": <bool>, "questions": [
    { "question": "<text>", "header": "<=12 chars>", "multiSelect": <bool>,
      "options": [ {"label": "<text>", "description": "<text>"}, ... ] }, ... ],
    "prototype": { "kind": "html | mermaid | ascii | table", "paths": ["<artifact path>", ...],
      "screenshot": "<path or null>", "inline_preview": "<text or null>",
      "variants": [ {"label": "<short>", "path": "<path or null>", "inline_preview": "<text or null>"} ] } }

The MEDIUM is the subagent's discretion: UI / layout / visual / game work → a WORKING
single-file HTML prototype with realistic mock data (the DEFAULT); backend / data / API /
refactor work → a mermaid diagram, an ASCII mockup, or a sample input/output table — whatever
communicates intent fastest. The guarantee is "always SOME visual", never "always HTML". It
may produce 2-4 labeled variants when the right direction is genuinely ambiguous. Screenshots
(chrome-devtools) are BEST-EFFORT: if unavailable or erroring it must skip them
(screenshot: null) without failing.

When `intent_clear` is true, `questions` is empty. When it is false, `questions` holds 1–4
decision-forcing questions (each with 2–4 options). This MUST be a FAST, lightweight clarification
that converges in ONE short turn.

Step 2 — PARSE and DECIDE. Read (JSON.parse) the object the subagent returned:

  - If `intent_clear` is false, YOU (the main agent — NOT the subagent) ask its `questions` to the
    user using the **AskUserQuestion** tool ONCE, mapping each question/header/multiSelect and its
    options (label + description) directly into AskUserQuestion's question format. AskUserQuestion
    is the MAIN agent's job; the subagent must never call it. Incorporate the user's answers.
  - If `intent_clear` is true, proceed without asking the user anything.

Step 3 — FINALIZE. Your final message MUST be the CONCISE confirmed INTENT as CLEAN PROSE — a
short paragraph stating the goal, key constraints, and success criteria (never the raw JSON,
no markdown) — and then, AS THE VERY LAST CONTENT of that final message, EXACTLY ONE of:

  - when the subagent returned a `prototype` object, this block with the subagent's
    `prototype` JSON object copied VERBATIM as its body:

---PROTOTYPE---
{the subagent's `prototype` JSON object, verbatim}
---END-PROTOTYPE---

  - or, when it returned no `prototype`, the single literal line:

NO-PROTOTYPE

Nothing may follow the block (or the line). Do not call any other tool after stating the intent.

content.text:
A web-based 2D arcade game where the player pilots an asteroid on an endless, open sandbox field — no win condition, just free play to experiment with the core mechanics. Size is the central rule: a sufficiently larger asteroid consumes a smaller one, growing its mass by the eaten mass minus a debris-loss penalty that scales with relative impact speed; two asteroids of roughly equal size mutually shatter on collision. A strong, arcade-tuned exponential gravity mechanic governs movement — large asteroids exert pull that grows exponentially with mass and bends the free-flight drift paths of the surrounding non-player asteroids, all of which are pure physics drifters (no AI, no networking) moved only by gravity and collisions. Gravity is tuned for fun (noticeable pull, slingshot maneuvers) rather than Newtonian realism or inescapable black-hole wells. The deliverable validates the consumption + exponential-gravity feel; the production build should target a 2D game engine (Phaser or PixiJS) for structure, physics helpers, and asset pipeline. Success = the consume/shatter rules and the gravity-warped drift visibly and enjoyably work in-browser at interactive frame rates.

---PROTOTYPE---

content.text:
Chrome DevTools MCP tools are not available in this session, so I'll skip the screenshot per the guard (set screenshot: null). The revision is complete.

Key tuning changes made to address the verbatim feedback:

- Pace slowed: gravity constant `G` 0.9 → 0.22, gravity exponent 1.35 → 1.18, gravity softening +400 → +2600 (so near-field pull is finite, never inescapable), drift velocity ±1.2 → ±0.45, thrust 0.18 → 0.11 with a speed cap of 6, mild drag on non-player bodies, calmer camera lerp.
- No instant death: the three huge 900–1800-mass gravity wells were removed entirely; a `SAFE_R=420` buffer guarantees nothing spawns on top of the player at t=0; the seed forces ≥16 edible (smaller-than-player) rocks so the player can grow safely first.
- Size distribution weighted to small / giants rare: new `powerMass()` uses a power-law (`u^4.2 → 14..1400`), so most asteroids are tiny and a ~1400-mass giant only appears when the random draw is near 1. Respawns reuse the same power-law, so the field never fills with large rocks.
- All existing mechanics retained: consume-with-debris-loss, equal-size mutual shatter, gravity halos on big bodies (`m>500`), HUD, growth-based camera zoom, debris particles.

Keep the parallax starfield working in this infinite space — make it tile/wrap infinitely around the camera (procedurally repeat star layers based on camera position with modulo wrapping) so there's always a star field no matter how far the player travels. IMPORTANT: removing boundaries must not let asteroids drift away forever and empty the field — manage population relative to the camera/player (see point 2): cull bodies that drift far off-screen (well beyond the viewport) and respawn them off-screen to keep a steady density around the player. They must never spawn inside the visible area (and never right on top of / in front of the player), so a fresh asteroid can never appear and consume the player. Pick a spawn position in the band between the viewport edge and some margin beyond it, accounting for the current camera zoom. Keep the existing safe-spawn-distance-from-player guard as a backstop. Maintain a roughly constant on-screen density by topping up off-screen whenever the nearby count drops.

Keep ALL existing mechanics and tuning intact: consume, shatter, gentle non-runaway gravity, halos, HUD, camera zoom, debris, small-weighted size distribution, no-instant-death, sub-linear growth, space-like coasting motion, thruster trail, freeze-proofing (caps, NaN guards, bounded loops, try/catch).

content.text:
No syntax errors. The script parses cleanly.

I've refined the existing prototype. The key changes addressing the two pieces of feedback:

**1. Infinite world (boundaries removed):**
- Deleted the soft-wrap clamp block (old lines 186-189: `if(A.x<-pad)A.x=W+pad...`) that teleported bodies back into a fixed `W x H` rectangle. Integration is now pure world-space — no walls, no clamps. Player position is never clamped or teleported.
- Replaced fixed-rect wrapping with **camera-relative cull & respawn**: a `viewRect()` helper computes the visible world rectangle from `cam.x/cam.y` and `cam.z`. Drifters that wander past `CULL_MARGIN` (1400 world-units beyond the viewport) are culled (player exempted via `if(A.player)return true`), and the field tops back up off-screen — so density travels with the camera into infinity without the field emptying.
- **Starfield tiles infinitely**: each parallax layer is a `W x H` star tile repeated via modulo wrapping on `cam` position (with positive-modulo `((v%t)+t)%t` so it works for negative/large coordinates in any direction). Stars are always present no matter how far you fly.

**2. Off-screen spawning:**
- New `offscreenPos()` picks a world position in a ring just beyond the viewport edge (`SPAWN_MARGIN`=160 to `SPAWN_MARGIN+SPAWN_BAND`), choosing a random side and sliding along it. Guaranteed outside the visible area, so a fresh rock can never appear in front of / on top of the player.
- `spawnDrifter()` now spawns off-screen and biases velocity gently inward toward the camera centre so rocks drift into view. The `SAFE_R` distance-from-player guard is retained as a backstop (bounded 40 tries + guaranteed fallback).
- Initial seed (including the edible-rock top-up) and all respawns route through off-screen spawning. `CULL_MARGIN > SPAWN_MARGIN` so freshly spawned rocks aren't instantly culled.
- `seed()` is now called after `cam`/`asts` are declared so spawn logic can read the viewport.

All existing mechanics/tuning preserved verbatim: consume (sub-linear damped growth + speed-loss penalty), mutual shatter, gentle non-runaway gravity, large-asteroid halos, HUD, camera zoom, debris/thruster particles, power-law small-weighted distribution, no-instant-death, frictionless coasting, and all freeze-proofing (body/particle caps, NaN `fin()` guards, bounded spawn loops, try/catch frame loop).

Note: chrome-devtools MCP was unavailable in this session, so no screenshot was captured (`screenshot: null`); the inline script passed a Node syntax check.

USER FEEDBACK (verbatim — this is the crux, read carefully):
"You're not understanding what I mean by collision physics. I'm not talking about a little sparkler or firework. I mean thing should break, deform, and reform when the collision is slow enough. You know… physics!"

INTERPRETATION — the user wants REAL, OUTCOME-CHANGING collision physics, not cosmetic particle sparkles. The collision OUTCOME must depend on relative impact velocity along a continuum:
- SLOW collision (low relative velocity): bodies should DEFORM and REFORM/COALESCE — i.e. they squish on contact and merge/accrete into one larger rounded body (gentle accretion), or bounce softly and settle, rather than exploding. Think soft-body / inelastic merge: two slow rocks touching gently stick together and reform into a single bigger asteroid. The asteroids should visibly deform (squash/stretch on contact) and then relax back to a rounded shape ("reform").
- MEDIUM collision: partial break — chunks chip off, bodies dent/crack but mostly survive, some momentum transfer/bounce (elastic-ish), maybe one cracks into a couple of pieces.
- FAST collision (high relative velocity): full FRACTURE — bodies actually BREAK into multiple real asteroid pieces (new bodies with conserved-ish momentum that fly apart), not just decorative debris particles. Shattering produces actual smaller asteroid bodies you can interact with.

Key requirements:
1. Make collision OUTCOME a function of relative velocity: slow = deform+merge/coalesce, medium = chip/crack+bounce, fast = fracture into multiple real bodies. Use clear named velocity thresholds (constants).
2. DEFORMATION must be VISIBLE: on contact, bodies squash along the impact axis (scale/stretch the rendered shape based on penetration & impact speed) and then elastically RELAX back toward their rest (rounded) shape over a short time — a spring/damping "reform". This deform-and-reform should be apparent even on bounces, not only merges.
3. BREAKING means real bodies: fast fractures spawn actual asteroid bodies (with mass conserved from the parent, split among fragments minus a debris-loss penalty), each with its own velocity, that participate in gravity/consume/collision — distinct from the cosmetic spark/debris particles which can remain as flavor on top.
4. Conserve momentum reasonably across merges, bounces, and fractures (inelastic for merges, partially elastic for bounces). Asteroids should have a restitution/elasticity feel.
5. Reconcile with existing size rules: the "much-bigger consumes much-smaller" rule and "roughly-equal mutually destroy" rule still apply, but now the *manner* of resolution is velocity-dependent — e.g. a slow approach between comparable sizes coalesces/accretes into a bigger body (reform), while a fast comparable-size impact fractures both. A big body catching a small one slowly accretes it smoothly (deform+absorb); catching it fast chips violent debris and real fragments. Make the consume/shatter mechanics read as physical consequences of these collision dynamics.

This is a real physics upgrade — soft-body-style deform/reform, velocity-thresholded break vs merge, and real fragment bodies — NOT more particle effects. Keep particles only as secondary flavor.

Preserve ALL existing systems: infinite world, off-screen-only spawning, cull/respawn, tiled starfield, gentle non-runaway gravity, halos, HUD, camera zoom, space-like coasting + thruster trail, small-weighted size distribution, no-instant-death, and ALL freeze-proofing (body cap, particle cap with drop-oldest, NaN guards, bounded spawn-placement loops, deferred fragment buffer so you never mutate the bodies array mid-iteration, try/catch frame loop). Fractures must respect the body cap (cap fragments-per-collision; if at the cap, produce fewer real bodies and fall back to particles).

---END-VISUAL-MODE---

Use the **plan-sizer** subagent to decide how to decompose the request, given the recon report.
Pass the recon report along with this decomposition-bias block:

---DECOMPOSITION-BIAS---
Greenfield projects (recon verdict: `non-repo`) with multiple subsystem concerns (rendering,
physics, controls, UI, persistence, networking, audio, asset loading, etc.) should default to
`split`.

**Quantitative rule:** if the recon verdict is `non-repo` AND the request implicates 2 or more
of those subsystems, the decision MUST be `split` unless the user's request contains an explicit
scope-narrowing clause like "just X", "only Y", or "minimal Z".

A `single` decision is only appropriate when:

input.content:
# Master Plan: Chompy Asteroids — Web 2D Asteroid Game

## Context

The goal is a web-based 2D arcade game where the player pilots an asteroid through an
infinite, boundary-less sandbox. Size is the core rule: much-bigger asteroids consume
much-smaller ones (mass grows by the eaten mass minus high-velocity debris loss);
roughly-equal asteroids destroy each other. A gentle, arcade-tuned **exponential** gravity
field from large bodies bends the free-flight paths of surrounding pure-physics drifters.

The headline mechanic is **velocity-thresholded collision physics** measured along the
contact normal:
- **Slow** closing speed → bodies deform (squash along the impact axis) then coalesce via an
  inelastic, momentum-conserving merge/accretion into one larger rounded body that springs
  back to shape.
- **Medium** → chip off a small real fragment, permanently dent both silhouettes, bounce with
  restitution (no sticking).
- **Fast** → fracture into multiple real asteroid sub-bodies that inherit parent momentum and
  burst outward, losing some mass to dust. Fragments are real bodies that gravitate, consume,
  and collide.

Every body carries a soft-body squash-and-reform spring so deformation reads on both bounces
and merges; particle effects are secondary flavor only.

**Current state (greenfield + reference prototype):** The working directory contains only
`.plan-tree/` metadata and a single-file Canvas prototype at
`.plan-tree/prototype/index.html` (~570 lines, all systems inline as globals). There is no
source tree, `package.json`, build tooling, engine dependency, or git. The prototype is
**load-bearing as the executable spec** — it already implements every headline mechanic and
the full anti-freeze suite, and every sub-plan should reference it.

**Resolved decision — exponential gravity:** The original request explicitly asks for
"exponential more gravity" for large asteroids. The prototype currently ships `GRAV_EXP = 1.0`
(linear) for calm pacing. The production build will honor the request: gravity force scales
with mass raised to a tunable exponent `GRAV_EXP > 1`, but kept **gently tuned** (softening,
per-body acceleration cap, global velocity cap) so large bodies bend drift paths without
creating inescapable runaway wells. `GRAV_EXP` is a named, tunable constant; the exact value
is a balancing decision in the final sub-plan.

**Engine decision (to be finalized in Sub-Plan 01):** The prototype's physics is hand-rolled
and engine-agnostic. **PixiJS (render-only, keep the custom simulation)** is the recommended
fit because Phaser's Arcade/Matter physics would fight the bespoke velocity-regime collision
model. Sub-Plan 01 confirms this and scaffolds accordingly.

## Success Criteria

Collisions visibly break, deform, bounce, and reform/merge as a continuous function of impact
speed, with real fragment bodies and conserved-ish momentum; exponential-but-gentle gravity
visibly bends drifter paths; the game runs enjoyably in-browser at stable frame rates with no
freezes or runaway physics. Core physics math is covered by unit tests.

## Architecture (Volatility-Based Decomposition)

Map the systems onto the standard split, drawn fresh since the prototype has no boundaries:
- **Connectors/** — input (keyboard), the PixiJS render adapter, the HUD/DOM overlay.
- **Operations/** — pure simulation logic: gravity, collision resolution, soft-body spring,
  growth/consume math, size distribution.
- **Workflows/** — the per-frame sim-step orchestration and the world/population manager.

The simulation state (the `Body` data structure: position, velocity, mass, radius,
soft-body deformation state, polygon shape) is the shared contract threaded through all plans.

---

### Sub-Plan 01: Engine scaffold, body model & render loop

Stand up the greenfield project and the rendering spine.
- Scaffold: package manager, bundler/dev-server (e.g. Vite), and **engine choice — confirm
  PixiJS (render-only) vs Phaser**, recording the rationale. Initialize git.
- Establish the `Workflows/ · Operations/ · Connectors/` module layout.
- Define the canonical **`Body`** data structure (world-space position, velocity, mass,
  derived radius, soft-body deformation state `{def, dvel, axis}`, polygon/shape) — this is
  the contract every later plan consumes.
- Build the camera + per-frame render loop that draws asteroid bodies (with soft-body
  squash applied to the rendered polygon), the infinitely tiled parallax starfield, and a HUD
  shell — all wrapped in the try/catch frame loop from the prototype.
- **Non-goals:** physics math, collision regimes, gravity, input handling, world
  population/spawn logic (stub bodies / mock data are fine for rendering).
- **Verification:** dev server runs; a handful of mock bodies render and the camera follows a
  designated body; starfield tiles correctly when the camera moves far in any direction;
  no console errors over a sustained run.

### Sub-Plan 02: Physics core — gravity, collision regimes, soft-body & growth

Rebuild the bespoke simulation as **pure, unit-testable modules** under `Operations/`.
- **Gravity:** softened N-body attraction with `GRAV_EXP > 1` (exponential mass scaling),
  per-body acceleration cap and global velocity cap, NaN/coincident-center guards.
- **Velocity-thresholded collision resolution** keyed on the normal component of relative
  velocity, with named thresholds `V_MERGE` / `V_FRACTURE`:
  - slow → deform + inelastic momentum-conserving merge/accretion;
  - medium → chip one real fragment + dent + restitution bounce (1D normal impulse);
  - fast → fracture into 2–N real fragment bodies inheriting parent momentum, with debris
    mass loss.
- **Soft-body** squash/reform damped-spring step per body.
- **Size rules as physical consequences:** big-eats-small and equal-destroy emerge from the
  regime logic; consume growth uses the diminishing-returns curve + high-velocity debris loss.
- **Interface contract:** consumes the `Body` structure and the per-frame update hook from
  Sub-Plan 01. Produces pure functions, e.g. `gravityAccel(bodies)`,
  `resolveCollision(a, b, relVelNormal) -> {regime, fragments, ...}`, `softBodyStep(body, dt)`,
  `applyConsume(...)`.
- **Non-goals:** rendering, input, world spawn/cull, HUD.
- **Verification (TDD, invariant-first):** unit tests asserting *intended* invariants —
  momentum conserved across a slow merge; total mass conserved minus the specified debris loss
  on fracture; regime selection at threshold boundaries (`< V_MERGE`, between, `>= V_FRACTURE`)
  including the exact boundary values; gravity acceleration respects the cap and never returns
  NaN for coincident centers; exponential scaling produces strictly greater force for a larger
  mass at equal distance. Each test must be shown to fail when the behavior is inverted.

### Sub-Plan 03: Input, controls & infinite-world population management

Player control and the systems that keep an infinite world populated and stable.
- **Controls:** frictionless space-like coasting (thrust = pure acceleration, momentum
  conserved on release), low velocity cap, thruster exhaust trail; camera follow.
- **World management (`Workflows/WorldManager`):** infinite boundary-less world,
  **off-screen-only** spawning in a ring beyond the viewport, camera-relative cull-and-respawn
  to hold steady density (player never culled), power-law **small-weighted** size
  distribution, guaranteed safe-spawn distance from player (no instant death).
- **Anti-freeze suite:** body cap with fragment-to-particle fallback, particle cap with
  drop-oldest, bounded spawn-placement loops with guaranteed fallback, deferred fragment
  buffer so the bodies array is never mutated mid-iteration.
- **Interface contract:** consumes the `Body` structure + physics step functions from
  Sub-Plan 02 and the camera/render loop from Sub-Plan 01. Produces `spawn()`,
  `cullAndRespawn(camera)`, `applyThrust(playerBody, inputState)`.
- **Non-goals:** physics math (consumed), render internals, final HUD polish.
- **Verification:** travel far in any direction — density stays roughly constant, nothing
  spawns inside the viewport, the field never empties; sustained run stays within body/particle
  caps (instrument counts); player survives the opening; thrust feels like coasting (no drag).

### Sub-Plan 04: HUD, integration & tuning

Wire the full vertical slice together, surface state, and balance the game.
- **Integration:** assemble the complete loop (input → gravity → collisions → growth →
  world management → render) into a single playable build.
- **HUD/UI:** mass/size readout, eaten/merged/fractured counters, growth indicator; optional
  debug overlays visualizing gravity influence and collision-regime classification.
- **Tuning:** balance the named constants against the prototype as spec — `GRAV_EXP` (and
  gravity softening / caps), `V_MERGE`, `V_FRACTURE`, restitution, debris mass-loss, growth
  curve, spawn density and size-distribution weighting — for the calm, survivable,
  non-runaway feel.
- **Interface contract:** consumes `WorldManager` (spawn/cullAndRespawn/applyThrust) and the
  player `Body` from Sub-Plan 03, the physics step functions from Sub-Plan 02, and the HUD
  shell + render loop + starfield from Sub-Plan 01.
- **Non-goals:** new subsystems — this is integration, surfacing, and balancing only.
- **Verification (end-to-end):** run the dev server and play; confirm each collision regime is
  reachable and visibly distinct (slow merge, medium chip+bounce, fast fracture into real
  bodies); confirm exponential gravity visibly bends drifter paths without runaway; confirm
  stable frame rate over a multi-minute session with no freeze and no NaN/explosion; HUD
  reflects live state.

## Execution Notes

- Sub-plans are **sequential**: 01 → 02 → 03 → 04, each consuming the prior's named artifacts.
- The throwaway `.plan-tree/prototype/index.html` is the reference spec for **all four**
  sub-plans — port its tuning values and anti-freeze patterns rather than reinventing them.
- Each sub-plan gets its own detailed plan via `/write-plan` before implementation.

- **Medium** → chip off a small real fragment, permanently dent both silhouettes, bounce with
  restitution (no sticking).
- **Fast** → fracture into multiple real asteroid sub-bodies that inherit parent momentum and
  burst outward, losing some mass to dust. Fragments are real bodies that gravitate, consume,
  and collide.

Every body carries a soft-body squash-and-reform spring so deformation reads on both bounces
and merges; particle effects are secondary flavor only.

**Current state (greenfield + reference prototype):** The working directory contains only
`.plan-tree/` metadata and a single-file Canvas prototype at
`.plan-tree/prototype/index.html` (~570 lines, all systems inline as globals). There is no
source tree, `package.json`, build tooling, engine dependency, or git. The prototype is
**load-bearing as the executable spec** — it already implements every headline mechanic and
the full anti-freeze suite, and every sub-plan should reference it.

**Resolved decision — exponential gravity:** The original request explicitly asks for
"exponential more gravity" for large asteroids. The prototype currently ships `GRAV_EXP = 1.0`
(linear) for calm pacing. The production build will honor the request: gravity force scales
with mass raised to a tunable exponent `GRAV_EXP > 1`, but kept **gently tuned** (softening,
per-body acceleration cap, global velocity cap) so large bodies bend drift paths without
creating inescapable runaway wells. `GRAV_EXP` is a named, tunable constant; the exact value
is a balancing decision in the final sub-plan.

**Engine decision (to be finalized in Sub-Plan 01):** The prototype's physics is hand-rolled
and engine-agnostic. **PixiJS (render-only, keep the custom simulation)** is the recommended
fit because Phaser's Arcade/Matter physics would fight the bespoke velocity-regime collision
model. Sub-Plan 01 confirms this and scaffolds accordingly.

## Success Criteria

Collisions visibly break, deform, bounce, and reform/merge as a continuous function of impact
speed, with real fragment bodies and conserved-ish momentum; exponential-but-gentle gravity
visibly bends drifter paths; the game runs enjoyably in-browser at stable frame rates with no
freezes or runaway physics. Core physics math is covered by unit tests.

## Architecture (Volatility-Based Decomposition)

Map the systems onto the standard split, drawn fresh since the prototype has no boundaries:
- **Connectors/** — input (keyboard), the PixiJS render adapter, the HUD/DOM overlay.
- **Operations/** — pure simulation logic: gravity, collision resolution, soft-body spring,
  growth/consume math, size distribution.
- **Workflows/** — the per-frame sim-step orchestration and the world/population manager.

The simulation state (the `Body` data structure: position, velocity, mass, radius,
soft-body deformation state, polygon shape) is the shared contract threaded through all plans.

---

### Sub-Plan 01: Engine scaffold, body model & render loop

Stand up the greenfield project and the rendering spine.
- Scaffold: package manager, bundler/dev-server (e.g. Vite), and **engine choice — confirm
  PixiJS (render-only) vs Phaser**, recording the rationale. Initialize git.
- Establish the `Workflows/ · Operations/ · Connectors/` module layout.
- Define the canonical **`Body`** data structure (world-space position, velocity, mass,
  derived radius, soft-body deformation state `{def, dvel, axis}`, polygon/shape) — this is
  the contract every later plan consumes.
- Build the camera + per-frame render loop that draws asteroid bodies (with soft-body
  squash applied to the rendered polygon), the infinitely tiled parallax starfield, and a HUD
  shell — all wrapped in the try/catch frame loop from the prototype.
- **Non-goals:** physics math, collision regimes, gravity, input handling, world
  population/spawn logic (stub bodies / mock data are fine for rendering).
- **Verification:** dev server runs; a handful of mock bodies render and the camera follows a
  designated body; starfield tiles correctly when the camera moves far in any direction;
  no console errors over a sustained run.

### Sub-Plan 02: Physics core — gravity, collision regimes, soft-body & growth

Rebuild the bespoke simulation as **pure, unit-testable modules** under `Operations/`.
- **Gravity:** softened N-body attraction with `GRAV_EXP > 1` (exponential mass scaling),
  per-body acceleration cap and global velocity cap, NaN/coincident-center guards.
- **Velocity-thresholded collision resolution** keyed on the normal component of relative
  velocity, with named thresholds `V_MERGE` / `V_FRACTURE`:
  - slow → deform + inelastic momentum-conserving merge/accretion;
  - medium → chip one real fragment + dent + restitution bounce (1D normal impulse);
  - fast → fracture into 2–N real fragment bodies inheriting parent momentum, with debris
    mass loss.
- **Soft-body** squash/reform damped-spring step per body.
- **Size rules as physical consequences:** big-eats-small and equal-destroy emerge from the
  regime logic; consume growth uses the diminishing-returns curve + high-velocity debris loss.
- **Interface contract:** consumes the `Body` structure and the per-frame update hook from
  Sub-Plan 01. Produces pure functions, e.g. `gravityAccel(bodies)`,
  `resolveCollision(a, b, relVelNormal) -> {regime, fragments, ...}`, `softBodyStep(body, dt)`,
  `applyConsume(...)`.
- **Non-goals:** rendering, input, world spawn/cull, HUD.
- **Verification (TDD, invariant-first):** unit tests asserting *intended* invariants —
  momentum conserved across a slow merge; total mass conserved minus the specified debris loss
  on fracture; regime selection at threshold boundaries (`< V_MERGE`, between, `>= V_FRACTURE`)
  including the exact boundary values; gravity acceleration respects the cap and never returns
  NaN for coincident centers; exponential scaling produces strictly greater force for a larger
  mass at equal distance. Each test must be shown to fail when the behavior is inverted.

### Sub-Plan 03: Input, controls & infinite-world population management

Player control and the systems that keep an infinite world populated and stable.
- **Controls:** frictionless space-like coasting (thrust = pure acceleration, momentum
  conserved on release), low velocity cap, thruster exhaust trail; camera follow.
- **World management (`Workflows/WorldManager`):** infinite boundary-less world,
  **off-screen-only** spawning in a ring beyond the viewport, camera-relative cull-and-respawn
  to hold steady density (player never culled), power-law **small-weighted** size
  distribution, guaranteed safe-spawn distance from player (no instant death).
- **Anti-freeze suite:** body cap with fragment-to-particle fallback, particle cap with
  drop-oldest, bounded spawn-placement loops with guaranteed fallback, deferred fragment
  buffer so the bodies array is never mutated mid-iteration.
- **Interface contract:** consumes the `Body` structure + physics step functions from
  Sub-Plan 02 and the camera/render loop from Sub-Plan 01. Produces `spawn()`,
  `cullAndRespawn(camera)`, `applyThrust(playerBody, inputState)`.
- **Non-goals:** physics math (consumed), render internals, final HUD polish.
- **Verification:** travel far in any direction — density stays roughly constant, nothing
  spawns inside the viewport, the field never empties; sustained run stays within body/particle
  caps (instrument counts); player survives the opening; thrust feels like coasting (no drag).

### Sub-Plan 04: HUD, integration & tuning

Wire the full vertical slice together, surface state, and balance the game.
- **Integration:** assemble the complete loop (input → gravity → collisions → growth →
  world management → render) into a single playable build.
- **HUD/UI:** mass/size readout, eaten/merged/fractured counters, growth indicator; optional
  debug overlays visualizing gravity influence and collision-regime classification.
- **Tuning:** balance the named constants against the prototype as spec — `GRAV_EXP` (and
  gravity softening / caps), `V_MERGE`, `V_FRACTURE`, restitution, debris mass-loss, growth
  curve, spawn density and size-distribution weighting — for the calm, survivable,
  non-runaway feel.
- **Interface contract:** consumes `WorldManager` (spawn/cullAndRespawn/applyThrust) and the
  player `Body` from Sub-Plan 03, the physics step functions from Sub-Plan 02, and the HUD
  shell + render loop + starfield from Sub-Plan 01.
- **Non-goals:** new subsystems — this is integration, surfacing, and balancing only.
- **Verification (end-to-end):** run the dev server and play; confirm each collision regime is
  reachable and visibly distinct (slow merge, medium chip+bounce, fast fracture into real
  bodies); confirm exponential gravity visibly bends drifter paths without runaway; confirm
  stable frame rate over a multi-minute session with no freeze and no NaN/explosion; HUD
  reflects live state.

## Execution Notes

- Sub-plans are **sequential**: 01 → 02 → 03 → 04, each consuming the prior's named artifacts.
- The throwaway `.plan-tree/prototype/index.html` is the reference spec for **all four**
  sub-plans — port its tuning values and anti-freeze patterns rather than reinventing them.
- Each sub-plan gets its own detailed plan via `/write-plan` before implementation.

content.text:
I've reviewed both the master plan and the prototype it claims to spec. The prototype is the ground truth here, and it contradicts the plan in several material ways. Findings below.

## Concrete issues + recommended fixes

**1. The plan's interface contract for collision is structurally impossible as written — collision mutates shared state, it is not a pure function.**
Sub-Plan 02 specifies `resolveCollision(a, b, relVelNormal) -> {regime, fragments, ...}` as a pure function. But in the prototype, collision resolution mutates `big.m`, `big.vx/vy`, `small.dead`, calls `applyDeform` on both bodies, `dentVerts` (mutates `verts[]`), increments global `eaten/merged/shatter`, pushes particles/shocks, and appends to `newFrags`. A single collision touches body mass, velocity, deformation, silhouette, death flags, three global counters, particles, and shockwaves. The proposed signature returns fragments but hides every other side effect. If Sub-Plan 02 is built to that signature, Sub-Plan 04 integration will discover the function can't actually drive the sim and will need a redesign mid-stream.
**Fix:** Define the real contract now: `resolveCollision(a, b)` returns a full *effect descriptor* — `{ regime, survivors:[{id, mNew, vx, vy, deform}], dead:[id], fragments:[Body], counters:{eaten,merged,shatter}, fx:{debris, shock} }` — and have a Workflow apply it. Decide explicitly where the counters and FX live (Operations should not own `eaten++`). Pin this in the master plan since it is the cross-plan contract.

**2. The exponential-gravity decision is under-specified to the point of being a latent runaway-physics bug, and the plan's own success criterion can't be met by the prototype's gravity model.**
The prototype uses force `= G·B.m^GRAV_EXP / (d² + SOFTEN)` and applies it as a *direct velocity delta per frame*, capped per-pair at `GRAV_ACC_CAP=0.05`. Three problems the plan doesn't address: (a) Raising `GRAV_EXP > 1` against masses up to ~1400 (and growing unbounded via accretion) makes `m^EXP` explode — at EXP=2, m=1400 yields force ~2M before softening; the `GRAV_ACC_CAP` will then clamp essentially *every* large-body interaction to the cap, flattening the field back toward constant-pull and *destroying* the very "exponentially more gravity for big bodies" distinction the request wants. So the plan's two goals (exponential differentiation vs. the acc cap) are in direct tension and the plan doesn't acknowledge it. (b) Force is frame-rate-dependent (no `dt`); the plan elsewhere implies a real sim step with `dt` (`softBodyStep(body, dt)`), so gravity and soft-body would be on different time bases. (c) Mass is unbounded (accretion only grows it), so any super-linear gravity term has no stable upper bound by construction.
**Fix:** In the master plan, state the gravity model explicitly: normalize mass (e.g. `(m/M_REF)^EXP`) so the exponent changes *shape* not *scale*, choose softening/cap so the cap is rarely the active constraint for in-range bodies, make the whole sim `dt`-based, and add a tunable mass ceiling or radius-based gravity falloff. Note that "exponential" should mean the *ratio* of pulls between a big and small body grows with size — verify that property in a test, not raw force magnitude.

**3. "Momentum conserved across a slow merge" — the prototype's own merge code violates this invariant, so the TDD test as specified will fail against the spec.**
Sub-Plan 02 verification demands a test asserting "momentum conserved across a slow merge." But the prototype computes the survivor velocity using the *combined* momentum divided by *combined* mass (`px/mt`, `mt = big.m+small.m`), then sets `big.m = newM` where `newM < big.m+small.m` (absorption is `ABSORB_BASE·diminish`, ~0.58×). Result: final momentum = `newM · (combinedP/combinedMass) ≠ combinedP`. Momentum is *not* conserved — it's scaled down by `newM/mt`. The mass-loss-to-debris is real and intended, but then momentum conservation is mathematically incompatible with mass non-conservation in a single merged body unless ejecta carry the missing momentum. The plan treats "momentum conserved" and "mass conserved minus debris loss" as independent testable invariants; with this formula they conflict.
**Fix:** Decide the actual invariant. Either (a) conserve momentum exactly (`v = combinedP/newM`) and account mass loss as massless debris, or (b) conserve momentum only for the lost-mass-weighted ejecta and assert *that*. Write the invariant the physics *should* satisfy (per the CLAUDE.md invariant-first rule) and make the merge formula match it — don't reverse-engineer the test from the buggy prototype formula.

**4. Sequencing: Sub-Plan 02 (physics) is specced to be built and unit-tested before Sub-Plan 03 (world population), but the physics regime logic has hard dependencies on population/anti-freeze state that 02 "owns" only partially.**
The fracture/chip paths read `asts.length + newFrags.length < MAX_BODIES`, compute `room`, and fall back to particles at the cap. That's collision logic *and* population-cap logic fused together. The plan puts `MAX_BODIES`/anti-freeze in Sub-Plan 03 but the fragment-count math lives in Sub-Plan 02's `resolveCollision`. So 02 can't be fully implemented or tested without 03's cap contract.
**Fix:** Move the body-cap/room budget into the contract surfaced by 01 (it's part of the world/sim constants), or pass an explicit `budget` argument into `resolveCollision`. Otherwise 02 will stub it and 03 will retrofit, breaking the "sequential, each consumes prior artifacts" promise.

**5. Missing concern: the O(n²) gravity + O(n²) collision double-loop is the actual performance risk, and the plan's "stable frame rate" criterion never names it.**
With `MAX_BODIES=140`, that's ~19.6k pair iterations for gravity *plus* ~9.8k for collisions every frame — fine at 140, but the plan also wants growth/zoom-out and offers no spatial partitioning. If tuning ever raises the cap, or if the production engine adds per-body overhead, this is where frame rate dies. The plan lists "stable frame rate" as success criteria with no performance budget or algorithmic note.
**Fix:** Note the O(n²) in the master plan, set an explicit body-count budget tied to a target frame time, and flag spatial hashing as the known scaling lever (out of scope for the prototype port, but the contract should not preclude it).

**6. Missing concern: there is no game-loss / player-death model in the decomposition, but the prototype has one and it's load-bearing for UX.**
The prototype kills the player (fracture/absorb) and shows a "Consumed." game-over overlay. The plan's HUD/integration sub-plan mentions counters and growth but never the lose condition, the game-over screen, or restart. Success criteria say "runs enjoyably" but a survival game with no specified fail/restart flow will get built inconsistently.
**Fix:** Add player-death + game-over + restart explicitly to Sub-Plan 04 (or 03), including the invariant "player is never culled" (prototype enforces this) and "player can die only by collision regime."

**7. Testability gap: the plan promises threshold-boundary tests on `V_MERGE`/`V_FRACTURE` "exact boundary values," but the regime is selected on `vn` (abs normal component) while the *medium* branch then re-guards with `if(vA < 0)` (only resolves if closing).**
A body pair can be classified MEDIUM by `vn` yet skip the impulse because `vA >= 0` (separating). The boundary tests as specified (`< V_MERGE`, between, `>= V_FRACTURE`) won't catch this separating-vs-closing subtlety, which is a real correctness seam (a glancing/​separating contact in the medium band does push-out + chip but no bounce).
**Fix:** Add the closing-vs-separating axis to the test matrix, and clarify in the contract whether `resolveCollision` should even fire on separating pairs (the prototype still chips and dents them, which may be unintended).

**8. Decomposition wrinkle: soft-body deform is claimed as a pure `Operations/` module, but it's invoked from *inside* collision resolution and from the render path, and stores per-frame spring velocity on the Body.**
`applyDeform` is called by collisions; the spring integrate runs in the main step; the render reads `def/dax/day`. The plan calls `softBodyStep(body, dt)` pure — fine — but `applyDeform` (the *injection*) is collision-triggered and the contract omits it. This is a small interface item that will cause friction.
**Fix:** Add `applyDeform`/deform-injection to the contract surface in Sub-Plan 02 alongside `softBodyStep`, and confirm Body's deform fields (`def, dvel, dax, day, flash`) are in the canonical Body definition in Sub-Plan 01 (the plan lists `{def, dvel, axis}` — missing `flash`, and `axis` is actually two scalars `dax/day`).

**9. Minor but will bite: the plan says Sub-Plan 01 Body has `soft-body deformation state {def, dvel, axis}` and "polygon shape," but the prototype Body also carries `rot, vr, verts[], hue, flash, dead, id, player`.** The canonical contract is the most-shared artifact across all four plans; an incomplete field list in 01 forces every later plan to amend it.
**Fix:** Make Sub-Plan 01 enumerate the *complete* Body field set from the prototype, or explicitly mark which fields are render-only vs. sim, so the contract is frozen before 02 builds against it.

---

Overall: the decomposition's *layering* (Connectors/Operations/Workflows, sequential 01→04) is sound and the volatility split is reasonable. The material risks are (a) the pure-function collision contract that the prototype's mutate-everything reality cannot honor (#1), (b) the unresolved exponential-gravity scale/cap contradiction (#2), and (c) the momentum-vs-mass conservation invariant conflict that will make the headline TDD test unsatisfiable against the current formula (#3). Those three should be resolved in the master plan before any sub-plan goes to `/write-plan`, because all three are cross-plan contract decisions, not implementation details.

I did not write a plan file — this was a read-only review. Relevant files: `/Users/user/Documents/repos/scratch/chompy-asteroids/.plan-tree/cli-plans/confirmed-intent-from-clarification-pure-truffle.md` and `/Users/user/Documents/repos/scratch/chompy-asteroids/.plan-tree/prototype/index.html`.

**Resolved decision — exponential gravity:** The original request explicitly asks for
"exponential more gravity" for large asteroids. The prototype currently ships `GRAV_EXP = 1.0`
(linear) for calm pacing. The production build will honor the request: gravity force scales
with mass raised to a tunable exponent `GRAV_EXP > 1`, but kept **gently tuned** (softening,
per-body acceleration cap, global velocity cap) so large bodies bend drift paths without
creating inescapable runaway wells. `GRAV_EXP` is a named, tunable constant; the exact value
is a balancing decision in the final sub-plan.
input.new_string:
**Resolved decision — exponential gravity (normalized, dt-based):** The original request
explicitly asks for "exponential more gravity" for large asteroids. The prototype ships
`GRAV_EXP = 1.0` (linear) for calm pacing. The production build honors the request but fixes a
real scale-vs-cap contradiction the prototype would hit: raising `GRAV_EXP > 1` against raw
masses (up to ~1400 and growing unbounded via accretion) makes `m^EXP` explode, so the
per-body acceleration cap would clamp *every* large interaction and flatten the field back to
constant pull — destroying the very differentiation we want. Therefore the contract is:
- Gravity uses **normalized mass**: force ∝ `(m / M_REF)^GRAV_EXP`, so the exponent changes
  the *shape/ratio* of pulls, not the absolute scale.
- The whole simulation is **`dt`-based** (gravity, soft-body, integration share one time base);
  the prototype's per-frame velocity deltas are converted to accelerations × `dt`.
- Softening and caps are tuned so the acceleration cap is a rare safety backstop, not the
  active constraint for in-range bodies; a tunable **mass ceiling** (or radius-based falloff)
  bounds the super-linear term.
- The intended invariant is **the ratio of pull between a large and small body grows with size**
  (tested directly), not raw force magnitude. `GRAV_EXP`, `M_REF`, softening, caps, and the
  mass ceiling are named tunables; final values are balanced in Sub-Plan 04.

The simulation state (the `Body` data structure: position, velocity, mass, radius,
soft-body deformation state, polygon shape) is the shared contract threaded through all plans.

### Frozen cross-plan contracts (resolved before any sub-plan goes to `/write-plan`)

These three are cross-plan decisions, not implementation details, so they are pinned here:

1. **Collision is NOT a side-effect-free pure function — it returns an effect descriptor that a
   Workflow applies.** The prototype's collision mutates mass, velocity, deformation, silhouette
   (dented verts), death flags, three global counters, particles, and shockwaves. Modeling that
   as `resolveCollision(a,b) -> {fragments}` would hide every other effect and force a redesign
   at integration. The real contract:
   `resolveCollision(a, b, budget) -> { regime, survivors:[{id, mNew, vx, vy, deformInjection}], dead:[id], fragments:[Body], counters:{eaten,merged,shatter}, fx:{debris, shock} }`.
   `Operations/` computes the descriptor (pure given inputs); a `Workflows/` step applies it to
   the world (mutates bodies, spawns fragments, bumps counters, pushes FX). Counters and FX are
   owned by the Workflow/HUD layer, never by `Operations`.
2. **Body-cap budget is an explicit input to collision, not hidden state.** Fracture/chip paths
   need the remaining body-count room to decide real-fragments-vs-particle-fallback. That room is
   passed in as `budget` (computed from `MAX_BODIES` and current population), so Sub-Plan 02 is
   fully implementable and testable without reaching into Sub-Plan 03's world state.
3. **Conservation invariant — momentum is conserved exactly; mass loss is massless debris.** On a
   slow merge the survivor velocity is `v = combinedMomentum / newMass` (NOT
   `combinedMomentum / combinedMass`), so momentum is conserved while mass drops by the debris
   loss; the lost mass is treated as massless ejecta/particles. This resolves the
   prototype's momentum≠conserved formula. Tests assert the invariant the physics *should*
   satisfy, not the prototype's current arithmetic.

**Performance note:** gravity and collision are both O(n²) pair loops. At `MAX_BODIES≈140`
(~20k + ~10k pairs/frame) this is fine, and it is the body-count budget that guarantees frame
rate. Spatial hashing is the known scaling lever if the cap ever rises — out of scope for the
port, but the contracts above must not preclude it.

  debris loss on fracture; regime selection across the full matrix — boundaries (`< V_MERGE`,
  between, `>= V_FRACTURE`) at exact values **crossed with closing-vs-separating** normal sign;
  fragment count respects the passed `budget` (falls back to particles at the cap); gravity
  acceleration respects the cap and never returns NaN for coincident centers; **the ratio of
  pull between a larger and smaller mass at equal distance strictly increases with `GRAV_EXP`**

input.plan:
# Master Plan: Chompy Asteroids — Web 2D Asteroid Game

## Context

The goal is a web-based 2D arcade game where the player pilots an asteroid through an
infinite, boundary-less sandbox. Size is the core rule: much-bigger asteroids consume
much-smaller ones (mass grows by the eaten mass minus high-velocity debris loss);
roughly-equal asteroids destroy each other. A gentle, arcade-tuned **exponential** gravity
field from large bodies bends the free-flight paths of surrounding pure-physics drifters.

The headline mechanic is **velocity-thresholded collision physics** measured along the
contact normal:
- **Slow** closing speed → bodies deform (squash along the impact axis) then coalesce via an
  inelastic, momentum-conserving merge/accretion into one larger rounded body that springs
  back to shape.
- **Medium** → chip off a small real fragment, permanently dent both silhouettes, bounce with
  restitution (no sticking).
- **Fast** → fracture into multiple real asteroid sub-bodies that inherit parent momentum and
  burst outward, losing some mass to dust. Fragments are real bodies that gravitate, consume,
  and collide.

Every body carries a soft-body squash-and-reform spring so deformation reads on both bounces
and merges; particle effects are secondary flavor only.

**Current state (greenfield + reference prototype):** The working directory contains only
`.plan-tree/` metadata and a single-file Canvas prototype at
`.plan-tree/prototype/index.html` (~570 lines, all systems inline as globals). There is no
source tree, `package.json`, build tooling, engine dependency, or git. The prototype is
**load-bearing as the executable spec** — it already implements every headline mechanic and
the full anti-freeze suite, and every sub-plan should reference it.

**Resolved decision — exponential gravity (normalized, dt-based):** The original request
explicitly asks for "exponential more gravity" for large asteroids. The prototype ships
`GRAV_EXP = 1.0` (linear) for calm pacing. The production build honors the request but fixes a
real scale-vs-cap contradiction the prototype would hit: raising `GRAV_EXP > 1` against raw
masses (up to ~1400 and growing unbounded via accretion) makes `m^EXP` explode, so the
per-body acceleration cap would clamp *every* large interaction and flatten the field back to
constant pull — destroying the very differentiation we want. Therefore the contract is:
- Gravity uses **normalized mass**: force ∝ `(m / M_REF)^GRAV_EXP`, so the exponent changes
  the *shape/ratio* of pulls, not the absolute scale.
- The whole simulation is **`dt`-based** (gravity, soft-body, integration share one time base);
  the prototype's per-frame velocity deltas are converted to accelerations × `dt`.
- Softening and caps are tuned so the acceleration cap is a rare safety backstop, not the
  active constraint for in-range bodies; a tunable **mass ceiling** (or radius-based falloff)
  bounds the super-linear term.
- The intended invariant is **the ratio of pull between a large and small body grows with size**
  (tested directly), not raw force magnitude. `GRAV_EXP`, `M_REF`, softening, caps, and the
  mass ceiling are named tunables; final values are balanced in Sub-Plan 04.

**Engine decision (to be finalized in Sub-Plan 01):** The prototype's physics is hand-rolled
and engine-agnostic. **PixiJS (render-only, keep the custom simulation)** is the recommended
fit because Phaser's Arcade/Matter physics would fight the bespoke velocity-regime collision
model. Sub-Plan 01 confirms this and scaffolds accordingly.

## Success Criteria

Collisions visibly break, deform, bounce, and reform/merge as a continuous function of impact
speed, with real fragment bodies and conserved-ish momentum; exponential-but-gentle gravity
visibly bends drifter paths; the game runs enjoyably in-browser at stable frame rates with no
freezes or runaway physics. Core physics math is covered by unit tests.

## Architecture (Volatility-Based Decomposition)

Map the systems onto the standard split, drawn fresh since the prototype has no boundaries:
- **Connectors/** — input (keyboard), the PixiJS render adapter, the HUD/DOM overlay.
- **Operations/** — pure simulation logic: gravity, collision resolution, soft-body spring,
  growth/consume math, size distribution.
- **Workflows/** — the per-frame sim-step orchestration and the world/population manager.

The simulation state (the `Body` data structure: position, velocity, mass, radius,
soft-body deformation state, polygon shape) is the shared contract threaded through all plans.

### Frozen cross-plan contracts (resolved before any sub-plan goes to `/write-plan`)

These three are cross-plan decisions, not implementation details, so they are pinned here:

1. **Collision is NOT a side-effect-free pure function — it returns an effect descriptor that a
   Workflow applies.** The prototype's collision mutates mass, velocity, deformation, silhouette
   (dented verts), death flags, three global counters, particles, and shockwaves. Modeling that
   as `resolveCollision(a,b) -> {fragments}` would hide every other effect and force a redesign
   at integration. The real contract:
   `resolveCollision(a, b, budget) -> { regime, survivors:[{id, mNew, vx, vy, deformInjection}], dead:[id], fragments:[Body], counters:{eaten,merged,shatter}, fx:{debris, shock} }`.
   `Operations/` computes the descriptor (pure given inputs); a `Workflows/` step applies it to
   the world (mutates bodies, spawns fragments, bumps counters, pushes FX). Counters and FX are
   owned by the Workflow/HUD layer, never by `Operations`.
2. **Body-cap budget is an explicit input to collision, not hidden state.** Fracture/chip paths
   need the remaining body-count room to decide real-fragments-vs-particle-fallback. That room is
   passed in as `budget` (computed from `MAX_BODIES` and current population), so Sub-Plan 02 is
   fully implementable and testable without reaching into Sub-Plan 03's world state.
3. **Conservation invariant — momentum is conserved exactly; mass loss is massless debris.** On a
   slow merge the survivor velocity is `v = combinedMomentum / newMass` (NOT
   `combinedMomentum / combinedMass`), so momentum is conserved while mass drops by the debris
   loss; the lost mass is treated as massless ejecta/particles. This resolves the
   prototype's momentum≠conserved formula. Tests assert the invariant the physics *should*
   satisfy, not the prototype's current arithmetic.

**Performance note:** gravity and collision are both O(n²) pair loops. At `MAX_BODIES≈140`
(~20k + ~10k pairs/frame) this is fine, and it is the body-count budget that guarantees frame
rate. Spatial hashing is the known scaling lever if the cap ever rises — out of scope for the
port, but the contracts above must not preclude it.

---

### Sub-Plan 01: Engine scaffold, body model & render loop

Stand up the greenfield project and the rendering spine.
- Scaffold: package manager, bundler/dev-server (e.g. Vite), and **engine choice — confirm
  PixiJS (render-only) vs Phaser**, recording the rationale. Initialize git.
- Establish the `Workflows/ · Operations/ · Connectors/` module layout.
- Define the canonical **`Body`** data structure — freeze the **complete** field set now (the
  prototype's: `id`, `player`, `dead`, world-space `x/y`, velocity `vx/vy`, `m` (mass), derived
  `r` (radius), rotation `rot`/`vr`, polygon `verts[]`, `hue`, and the soft-body deformation
  state `def, dvel, dax, day, flash`). Mark each field render-only vs. sim so the contract is
  frozen before Sub-Plan 02 builds against it (an incomplete list forces every later plan to
  amend the most-shared artifact).
- Build the camera + per-frame render loop that draws asteroid bodies (with soft-body
  squash applied to the rendered polygon), the infinitely tiled parallax starfield, and a HUD
  shell — all wrapped in the try/catch frame loop from the prototype.
- **Non-goals:** physics math, collision regimes, gravity, input handling, world
  population/spawn logic (stub bodies / mock data are fine for rendering).
- **Verification:** dev server runs; a handful of mock bodies render and the camera follows a
  designated body; starfield tiles correctly when the camera moves far in any direction;
  no console errors over a sustained run.

### Sub-Plan 02: Physics core — gravity, collision regimes, soft-body & growth

Rebuild the bespoke simulation as **pure, unit-testable modules** under `Operations/`.
- **Gravity:** softened N-body attraction with `GRAV_EXP > 1` (exponential mass scaling),
  per-body acceleration cap and global velocity cap, NaN/coincident-center guards.
- **Velocity-thresholded collision resolution** keyed on the normal component of relative
  velocity, with named thresholds `V_MERGE` / `V_FRACTURE`:
  - slow → deform + inelastic momentum-conserving merge/accretion;
  - medium → chip one real fragment + dent + restitution bounce (1D normal impulse);
  - fast → fracture into 2–N real fragment bodies inheriting parent momentum, with debris
    mass loss.
- **Soft-body** squash/reform damped-spring step per body, plus the **deform injection**
  (`applyDeform`-style) that collisions call to kick the spring — both belong to this module's
  contract surface (the injection is collision-triggered; the integrate runs in the step).
- **Size rules as physical consequences:** big-eats-small and equal-destroy emerge from the
  regime logic; consume growth uses the diminishing-returns curve + high-velocity debris loss.
- **Interface contract (per the Frozen contracts above):** consumes the `Body` structure and
  the `dt`-based per-frame update hook from Sub-Plan 01. Produces `gravityAccel(bodies, dt)`;
  `resolveCollision(a, b, budget) -> effectDescriptor` (the full descriptor shape, NOT a
  fragments-only return); `softBodyStep(body, dt)` + `applyDeform(...)`. Regime is selected on
  the normal component of relative velocity AND closing-vs-separating sign — separating pairs
  must follow an explicitly decided path (chip/dent or no-op), not fall through ambiguously.
- **Non-goals:** rendering, input, world spawn/cull, HUD, applying the effect descriptor.
- **Verification (TDD, invariant-first):** unit tests asserting *intended* invariants —
  **momentum conserved exactly across a slow merge** (`v = combinedMomentum/newMass`) while
  mass drops by the debris loss (massless ejecta); total mass conserved minus the specified
  debris loss on fracture; regime selection across the full matrix — boundaries (`< V_MERGE`,
  between, `>= V_FRACTURE`) at exact values **crossed with closing-vs-separating** normal sign;
  fragment count respects the passed `budget` (falls back to particles at the cap); gravity
  acceleration respects the cap and never returns NaN for coincident centers; **the ratio of
  pull between a larger and smaller mass at equal distance strictly increases with `GRAV_EXP`**
  (the exponential-differentiation invariant — test the ratio, not raw magnitude). Each test
  must be shown to fail when the behavior is inverted.

### Sub-Plan 03: Input, controls & infinite-world population management

Player control and the systems that keep an infinite world populated and stable.
- **Controls:** frictionless space-like coasting (thrust = pure acceleration, momentum
  conserved on release), low velocity cap, thruster exhaust trail; camera follow.
- **World management (`Workflows/WorldManager`):** infinite boundary-less world,
  **off-screen-only** spawning in a ring beyond the viewport, camera-relative cull-and-respawn
  to hold steady density (player never culled), power-law **small-weighted** size
  distribution, guaranteed safe-spawn distance from player (no instant death).
- **Anti-freeze suite:** body cap with fragment-to-particle fallback, particle cap with
  drop-oldest, bounded spawn-placement loops with guaranteed fallback, deferred fragment
  buffer so the bodies array is never mutated mid-iteration.
- **Interface contract:** consumes the `Body` structure + physics step functions from
  Sub-Plan 02 and the camera/render loop from Sub-Plan 01. Produces `spawn()`,
  `cullAndRespawn(camera)`, `applyThrust(playerBody, inputState)`.
- **Non-goals:** physics math (consumed), render internals, final HUD polish.
- **Verification:** travel far in any direction — density stays roughly constant, nothing
  spawns inside the viewport, the field never empties; sustained run stays within body/particle
  caps (instrument counts); player survives the opening; thrust feels like coasting (no drag).

### Sub-Plan 04: HUD, integration & tuning

Wire the full vertical slice together, surface state, and balance the game.
- **Integration:** assemble the complete loop (input → gravity → collisions → growth →
  world management → render) into a single playable build.
- **HUD/UI:** mass/size readout, eaten/merged/fractured counters, growth indicator; optional
  debug overlays visualizing gravity influence and collision-regime classification.
- **Lose/restart flow:** the player CAN die (consumed by a much-larger body, or fractured by a
  fast comparable impact) but is NEVER culled by world management — death is collision-only.
  Specify the game-over overlay ("Consumed.") and a restart that reseeds the world. ("No instant
  death" remains an opening-survivability guarantee, not immortality.)
- **Tuning:** balance the named constants against the prototype as spec — `GRAV_EXP` (and
  gravity softening / caps), `V_MERGE`, `V_FRACTURE`, restitution, debris mass-loss, growth
  curve, spawn density and size-distribution weighting — for the calm, survivable,
  non-runaway feel.
- **Interface contract:** consumes `WorldManager` (spawn/cullAndRespawn/applyThrust) and the
  player `Body` from Sub-Plan 03, the physics step functions from Sub-Plan 02, and the HUD
  shell + render loop + starfield from Sub-Plan 01.
- **Non-goals:** new subsystems — this is integration, surfacing, and balancing only.
- **Verification (end-to-end):** run the dev server and play; confirm each collision regime is
  reachable and visibly distinct (slow merge, medium chip+bounce, fast fracture into real
  bodies); confirm exponential gravity visibly bends drifter paths without runaway; confirm
  stable frame rate over a multi-minute session with no freeze and no NaN/explosion; HUD
  reflects live state.

## Execution Notes

- Sub-plans are **sequential**: 01 → 02 → 03 → 04, each consuming the prior's named artifacts.
- The throwaway `.plan-tree/prototype/index.html` is the reference spec for **all four**
  sub-plans — port its tuning values and anti-freeze patterns rather than reinventing them.
- Each sub-plan gets its own detailed plan via `/write-plan` before implementation.

Pass the recon report along with this decomposition-bias block:

---DECOMPOSITION-BIAS---
Greenfield projects (recon verdict: `non-repo`) with multiple subsystem concerns (rendering,
physics, controls, UI, persistence, networking, audio, asset loading, etc.) should default to
`split`.

**Quantitative rule:** if the recon verdict is `non-repo` AND the request implicates 2 or more
of those subsystems, the decision MUST be `split` unless the user's request contains an explicit
scope-narrowing clause like "just X", "only Y", or "minimal Z".

A `single` decision is only appropriate when:
- The work is genuinely single-volatility (one concern, one module), OR
- The user's request contains an explicit scope-narrowing clause (above), OR
- An existing codebase already establishes the cross-cutting layers and the new work is one
  concern within them.

When in doubt: lean split. A master plan with one or two sub-plans is easy to collapse if the
user wants; an oversized single plan is painful to retroactively decompose.
---END-DECOMPOSITION-BIAS---

After it returns, emit exactly one line at the top level of the form:

SIZER: <single|split> / <num_plans> / <confidence>

e.g. `SIZER: split / 3 / 0.82`. Those are the ONLY two decisions — when uncertain, choose
`split` (the master plan gate is the human checkpoint for an uncertain decomposition).

Emit nothing else after the SIZER line.

Draft the implementation plan for sub-plan 01. Its mandate from the master plan:

### Sub-Plan 01: Engine scaffold, body model & render loop

Stand up the greenfield project and the rendering spine.
- Scaffold: package manager, bundler/dev-server (e.g. Vite), and **engine choice — confirm
  PixiJS (render-only) vs Phaser**, recording the rationale. Initialize git.
- Establish the `Workflows/ · Operations/ · Connectors/` module layout.
- Define the canonical **`Body`** data structure — freeze the **complete** field set now (the
  prototype's: `id`, `player`, `dead`, world-space `x/y`, velocity `vx/vy`, `m` (mass), derived
  `r` (radius), rotation `rot`/`vr`, polygon `verts[]`, `hue`, and the soft-body deformation
  state `def, dvel, dax, day, flash`). Mark each field render-only vs. sim so the contract is
  frozen before Sub-Plan 02 builds against it (an incomplete list forces every later plan to
  amend the most-shared artifact).
- Build the camera + per-frame render loop that draws asteroid bodies (with soft-body
  squash applied to the rendered polygon), the infinitely tiled parallax starfield, and a HUD
  shell — all wrapped in the try/catch frame loop from the prototype.
- **Non-goals:** physics math, collision regimes, gravity, input handling, world
  population/spawn logic (stub bodies / mock data are fine for rendering).
- **Verification:** dev server runs; a handful of mock bodies render and the camera follows a
  designated body; starfield tiles correctly when the camera moves far in any direction;
  no console errors over a sustained run.

Master-plan preamble (shared context for every sub-plan):

# Master Plan: Chompy Asteroids — Web 2D Asteroid Game

## Context

The goal is a web-based 2D arcade game where the player pilots an asteroid through an
infinite, boundary-less sandbox. Size is the core rule: much-bigger asteroids consume
much-smaller ones (mass grows by the eaten mass minus high-velocity debris loss);
roughly-equal asteroids destroy each other. A gentle, arcade-tuned **exponential** gravity
field from large bodies bends the free-flight paths of surrounding pure-physics drifters.

The headline mechanic is **velocity-thresholded collision physics** measured along the
contact normal:
- **Slow** closing speed → bodies deform (squash along the impact axis) then coalesce via an
  inelastic, momentum-conserving merge/accretion into one larger rounded body that springs
  back to shape.
- **Medium** → chip off a small real fragment, permanently dent both silhouettes, bounce with
  restitution (no sticking).
- **Fast** → fracture into multiple real asteroid sub-bodies that inherit parent momentum and
  burst outward, losing some mass to dust. Fragments are real bodies that gravitate, consume,
  and collide.

Every body carries a soft-body squash-and-reform spring so deformation reads on both bounces
and merges; particle effects are secondary flavor only.

**Current state (greenfield + reference prototype):** The working directory contains only
`.plan-tree/` metadata and a single-file Canvas prototype at
`.plan-tree/prototype/index.html` (~570 lines, all systems inline as globals). There is no
source tree, `package.json`, build tooling, engine dependency, or git. The prototype is
**load-bearing as the executable spec** — it already implements every headline mechanic and
the full anti-freeze suite, and every sub-plan should reference it.

**Resolved decision — exponential gravity (normalized, dt-based):** The original request
explicitly asks for "exponential more gravity" for large asteroids. The prototype ships
`GRAV_EXP = 1.0` (linear) for calm pacing. The production build honors the request but fixes a
real scale-vs-cap contradiction the prototype would hit: raising `GRAV_EXP > 1` against raw
masses (up to ~1400 and growing unbounded via accretion) makes `m^EXP` explode, so the
per-body acceleration cap would clamp *every* large interaction and flatten the field back to
constant pull — destroying the very differentiation we want. Therefore the contract is:
- Gravity uses **normalized mass**: force ∝ `(m / M_REF)^GRAV_EXP`, so the exponent changes
  the *shape/ratio* of pulls, not the absolute scale.
- The whole simulation is **`dt`-based** (gravity, soft-body, integration share one time base);
  the prototype's per-frame velocity deltas are converted to accelerations × `dt`.
- Softening and caps are tuned so the acceleration cap is a rare safety backstop, not the
  active constraint for in-range bodies; a tunable **mass ceiling** (or radius-based falloff)
  bounds the super-linear term.
- The intended invariant is **the ratio of pull between a large and small body grows with size**
  (tested directly), not raw force magnitude. `GRAV_EXP`, `M_REF`, softening, caps, and the
  mass ceiling are named tunables; final values are balanced in Sub-Plan 04.

**Engine decision (to be finalized in Sub-Plan 01):** The prototype's physics is hand-rolled
and engine-agnostic. **PixiJS (render-only, keep the custom simulation)** is the recommended
fit because Phaser's Arcade/Matter physics would fight the bespoke velocity-regime collision
model. Sub-Plan 01 confirms this and scaffolds accordingly.

## Success Criteria

Collisions visibly break, deform, bounce, and reform/merge as a continuous function of impact
speed, with real fragment bodies and conserved-ish momentum; exponential-but-gentle gravity
visibly bends drifter paths; the game runs enjoyably in-browser at stable frame rates with no
freezes or runaway physics. Core physics math is covered by unit tests.

## Architecture (Volatility-Based Decomposition)

Map the systems onto the standard split, drawn fresh since the prototype has no boundaries:
- **Connectors/** — input (keyboard), the PixiJS render adapter, the HUD/DOM overlay.
- **Operations/** — pure simulation logic: gravity, collision resolution, soft-body spring,
  growth/consume math, size distribution.
- **Workflows/** — the per-frame sim-step orchestration and the world/population manager.

The simulation state (the `Body` data structure: position, velocity, mass, radius,
soft-body deformation state, polygon shape) is the shared contract threaded through all plans.

### Frozen cross-plan contracts (resolved before any sub-plan goes to `/write-plan`)

These three are cross-plan decisions, not implementation details, so they are pinned here:

1. **Collision is NOT a side-effect-free pure function — it returns an effect descriptor that a
   Workflow applies.** The prototype's collision mutates mass, velocity, deformation, silhouette
   (dented verts), death flags, three global counters, particles, and shockwaves. Modeling that
   as `resolveCollision(a,b) -> {fragments}` would hide every other effect and force a redesign
   at integration. The real contract:
   `resolveCollision(a, b, budget) -> { regime, survivors:[{id, mNew, vx, vy, deformInjection}], dead:[id], fragments:[Body], counters:{eaten,merged,shatter}, fx:{debris, shock} }`.
   `Operations/` computes the descriptor (pure given inputs); a `Workflows/` step applies it to
   the world (mutates bodies, spawns fragments, bumps counters, pushes FX). Counters and FX are
   owned by the Workflow/HUD layer, never by `Operations`.
2. **Body-cap budget is an explicit input to collision, not hidden state.** Fracture/chip paths
   need the remaining body-count room to decide real-fragments-vs-particle-fallback. That room is
   passed in as `budget` (computed from `MAX_BODIES` and current population), so Sub-Plan 02 is
   fully implementable and testable without reaching into Sub-Plan 03's world state.
3. **Conservation invariant — momentum is conserved exactly; mass loss is massless debris.** On a
   slow merge the survivor velocity is `v = combinedMomentum / newMass` (NOT
   `combinedMomentum / combinedMass`), so momentum is conserved while mass drops by the debris
   loss; the lost mass is treated as massless ejecta/particles. This resolves the
   prototype's momentum≠conserved formula. Tests assert the invariant the physics *should*
   satisfy, not the prototype's current arithmetic.

**Performance note:** gravity and collision are both O(n²) pair loops. At `MAX_BODIES≈140`
(~20k + ~10k pairs/frame) this is fine, and it is the body-count budget that guarantees frame
rate. Spatial hashing is the known scaling lever if the cap ever rises — out of scope for the
port, but the contracts above must not preclude it.

---

Run a silent **devils-advocate-reviewer** pass over the draft and incorporate its findings.
Then call **ExitPlanMode** with the full sub-plan as `plan` to hold for approval.
